pattern: For any candidate final value v, the modification cost is just a count of how many array entries share a factor with v, discounted by one if some entry already equals v, so a Mobius-style inclusion-exclusion over v's own prime factors turns checking every candidate into a fast sieve.
hints: For a fixed target value v at the chosen index, every OTHER entry that isn't already coprime with v must be changed (one operation each) -- so the total cost only depends on how many entries conflict with v, plus possibly one extra operation to actually create v somewhere.

If some entry already equals v exactly, use that entry as the chosen index for free; if not but at least one entry conflicts with v, redirect one of those already-needed changes straight to v instead of a generic fix; only when neither applies does creating v cost a full extra operation.

To count how many array entries are NOT coprime with v quickly for every v up to maxVal, precompute how many entries are divisible by each d via a sieve, then for each v use inclusion-exclusion over v's (at most a handful of) distinct prime factors to get the coprime count in one shot -- v itself does not have to be capped at maxVal if it is an existing untouched value.

edge_inputs.description: one element must be raised to reach a value coprime with the rest
no modification needed since a coprime element already exists
the best selected value can exceed maxVal by leaving an untouched element alone while fixing the rest
a single element array needs no changes at all
every element shares a common factor, forcing at least one change even though maxVal is small
maxVal equal to 1 forces every changed element down to the value 1
duplicates of the same large prime value are already pairwise non-coprime with each other

stress.description: random small array and maxVal to cross-check against the brute-force oracle
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 300))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 300) for _ in range(n)]
    maxVal = rng.randint(1, 300)
    return [nums, maxVal]

stress.description: larger array and maxVal, still small enough for the brute oracle to finish quickly
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1500))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1500) for _ in range(n)]
    maxVal = rng.randint(1, 1500)
    return [nums, maxVal]
